instruction: Demonstrate advanced Rust debugging techniques using RUST_BACKTRACE, cargo-expand, and the tracing crate for structured diagnostics in production applications, including span hierarchies and subscriber configuration.
output:
<think>
Rust debugging involves several layers: compile-time checks (the borrow checker catches most bugs), runtime diagnostics (panic messages, backtraces), and production observability (tracing).

Key tools:
1. RUST_BACKTRACE=1/full — shows call stack on panic
2. cargo-expand — shows expanded macros (essential for debugging proc macros and derive)
3. tracing crate — structured logging with spans, levels, and subscriber-based output
4. #[track_caller] — shows the caller's location in error messages
5. dbg! macro — quick debug printing that shows file/line/expression/value

For tracing:
- Spans: represent a period of time (entering/exiting a function)
- Events: discrete points (log messages within a span)
- Subscribers: consume and format the output (console, file, JSON, OpenTelemetry)
- Layers: composable subscribers for different outputs

I'll build:
1. A practical tracing setup with multiple layers
2. Custom span fields for request tracking
3. Async-aware tracing with tokio
4. Error handling that preserves context
5. Integration with panic hooks for complete crash reporting
</think>

```rust
// Cargo.toml dependencies:
// tracing = "0.1"
// tracing-subscriber = { version = "0.3", features = ["env-filter", "json", "fmt"] }
// tracing-appender = "0.2"
// color-eyre = "0.6"

use std::time::Instant;

// In production, use the actual crates. This shows the patterns.
// For this example, we'll use println to simulate tracing output.

// ===== Tracing setup (production pattern) =====

/*
// Real tracing setup:
use tracing::{info, warn, error, debug, instrument, span, Level};
use tracing_subscriber::{fmt, EnvFilter, layer::SubscriberExt, util::SubscriberInitExt};

fn init_tracing() {
    let fmt_layer = fmt::layer()
        .with_target(true)
        .with_thread_ids(true)
        .with_file(true)
        .with_line_number(true);

    let filter = EnvFilter::try_from_default_env()
        .unwrap_or_else(|_| EnvFilter::new("info"));

    // JSON output for production
    let json_layer = fmt::layer().json();

    tracing_subscriber::registry()
        .with(filter)
        .with(fmt_layer)
        // .with(json_layer)  // uncomment for JSON output
        .init();
}
*/

// ===== Simulated tracing for standalone example =====

mod trace_sim {
    use std::time::Instant;

    pub struct Span {
        name: &'static str,
        start: Instant,
        fields: Vec<(String, String)>,
    }

    impl Span {
        pub fn new(name: &'static str) -> Self {
            let span = Span {
                name,
                start: Instant::now(),
                fields: Vec::new(),
            };
            println!("[SPAN ENTER] {} {}", name,
                span.fields.iter().map(|(k,v)| format!("{}={}", k, v)).collect::<Vec<_>>().join(" "));
            span
        }

        pub fn with_field(mut self, key: &str, value: impl std::fmt::Display) -> Self {
            self.fields.push((key.to_string(), value.to_string()));
            self
        }
    }

    impl Drop for Span {
        fn drop(&mut self) {
            let elapsed = self.start.elapsed();
            println!("[SPAN EXIT]  {} ({:.3}ms)", self.name, elapsed.as_secs_f64() * 1000.0);
        }
    }

    pub fn info(msg: &str) { println!("[INFO]  {}", msg); }
    pub fn warn(msg: &str) { println!("[WARN]  {}", msg); }
    pub fn error(msg: &str) { println!("[ERROR] {}", msg); }
    pub fn debug(msg: &str) { println!("[DEBUG] {}", msg); }
}

// ===== Application code with tracing =====

#[derive(Debug)]
struct Request {
    id: String,
    path: String,
    method: String,
}

#[derive(Debug)]
enum AppError {
    NotFound(String),
    Internal(String),
    Timeout,
}

impl std::fmt::Display for AppError {
    fn fmt(&self, f: &mut std::fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> std::fmt::Result {
        match self {
            AppError::NotFound(r) => write!(f, "not found: {}", r),
            AppError::Internal(msg) => write!(f, "internal error: {}", msg),
            AppError::Timeout => write!(f, "request timeout"),
        }
    }
}

// #[instrument] equivalent — in real code, use the attribute macro
fn handle_request(req: &Request) -> Result<String, AppError> {
    let _span = trace_sim::Span::new("handle_request")
        .with_field("request_id", &req.id)
        .with_field("path", &req.path);

    trace_sim::info(&format!("Processing {} {}", req.method, req.path));

    // Simulate processing pipeline
    let auth = authenticate(&req.id)?;
    trace_sim::debug(&format!("Authenticated: {}", auth));

    let data = fetch_data(&req.path)?;
    trace_sim::info(&format!("Fetched {} bytes", data.len()));

    Ok(data)
}

fn authenticate(request_id: &str) -> Result<bool, AppError> {
    let _span = trace_sim::Span::new("authenticate");
    // Simulate auth check
    if request_id.contains("bad") {
        trace_sim::warn("Authentication failed");
        return Err(AppError::Internal("auth failed".to_string()));
    }
    Ok(true)
}

fn fetch_data(path: &str) -> Result<String, AppError> {
    let _span = trace_sim::Span::new("fetch_data")
        .with_field("path", path);

    if path.contains("404") {
        trace_sim::error(&format!("Resource not found: {}", path));
        return Err(AppError::NotFound(path.to_string()));
    }

    // Simulate work
    std::thread::sleep(std::time::Duration::from_millis(5));
    Ok(format!("data from {}", path))
}

// ===== RUST_BACKTRACE and panic handling =====

fn setup_panic_hook() {
    std::panic::set_hook(Box::new(|panic_info| {
        // Log the panic with full context
        let location = panic_info.location()
            .map(|l| format!("{}:{}:{}", l.file(), l.line(), l.column()))
            .unwrap_or_else(|| "unknown".to_string());

        let message = panic_info.payload()
            .downcast_ref::<&str>()
            .copied()
            .or_else(|| panic_info.payload().downcast_ref::<String>().map(|s| s.as_str()))
            .unwrap_or("unknown panic");

        eprintln!("\n=== PANIC ===");
        eprintln!("Location: {}", location);
        eprintln!("Message: {}", message);
        eprintln!("Thread: {:?}", std::thread::current().name());

        // Capture backtrace
        let bt = std::backtrace::Backtrace::force_capture();
        eprintln!("Backtrace:\n{}", bt);
    }));
}

// ===== #[track_caller] for better error locations =====

#[track_caller]
fn expect_positive(value: i32) -> i32 {
    if value <= 0 {
        let caller = std::panic::Location::caller();
        panic!("Expected positive value, got {} (called from {})", value, caller);
    }
    value
}

// ===== dbg! macro patterns =====

fn demonstrate_dbg() {
    let x = 42;
    let y = dbg!(x * 2);  // prints: [src/main.rs:N] x * 2 = 84
    let _ = y;

    // Chain with dbg!
    let result = dbg!(dbg!(2 + 3) * dbg!(4 + 5));
    // Prints each subexpression with its value
    let _ = result;
}

fn main() {
    setup_panic_hook();

    println!("=== Tracing Demo ===\n");

    // Successful request
    let req = Request {
        id: "req-001".into(),
        path: "/api/users".into(),
        method: "GET".into(),
    };
    match handle_request(&req) {
        Ok(data) => println!("Response: {}\n", data),
        Err(e) => println!("Error: {}\n", e),
    }

    // Failed request
    let req = Request {
        id: "req-002".into(),
        path: "/api/404/missing".into(),
        method: "GET".into(),
    };
    match handle_request(&req) {
        Ok(data) => println!("Response: {}\n", data),
        Err(e) => println!("Error: {}\n", e),
    }

    // dbg! patterns
    println!("\n=== dbg! Demo ===");
    demonstrate_dbg();

    // cargo-expand usage (shown as comment)
    println!("\n=== cargo-expand ===");
    println!("Usage: cargo expand --lib       # expand all macros");
    println!("       cargo expand module_name # expand specific module");
    println!("       cargo expand --test test_name # expand test macros");

    println!("\n=== RUST_BACKTRACE ===");
    println!("RUST_BACKTRACE=1 cargo run    # short backtrace");
    println!("RUST_BACKTRACE=full cargo run # full backtrace with file/line");
}
```

instruction: Demonstrate Miri usage for detecting undefined behavior in unsafe Rust code, including examples of common UB patterns it catches and how to structure tests for Miri validation.
output:
<think>
Miri is Rust's experimental interpreter that detects undefined behavior (UB) at runtime. It's invaluable for validating unsafe code.

What Miri catches:
1. Use-after-free
2. Out-of-bounds memory access
3. Use of uninitialized memory
4. Invalid references (dangling, misaligned)
5. Data races (with -Zmiri-check-number-validity)
6. Stacked Borrows violations (aliasing rules)
7. Memory leaks (with -Zmiri-leak-check)
8. Integer overflow in debug mode

How to use:
- Install: `rustup +nightly component add miri`
- Run: `cargo +nightly miri test`
- Flags: `MIRIFLAGS="-Zmiri-backtrace=full" cargo miri test`

I'll show:
1. Code that looks correct but has UB — Miri catches it
2. Stacked Borrows violations (the most common Miri finding)
3. How to structure tests specifically for Miri
4. Common patterns and their fixes

Stacked Borrows is Miri's aliasing model. It enforces that:
- A &mut reference is unique (no other references to the same location)
- Creating a raw pointer from a &mut and then using the &mut invalidates the raw pointer
- You can't use a raw pointer after the borrow it came from is invalidated

I'll write the code as test functions since that's how you'd actually use Miri.
</think>

```rust
// Run these tests with: cargo +nightly miri test
// Install miri: rustup +nightly component add miri

// ===== SAFE: Correct unsafe code that Miri validates =====

/// Safe raw pointer usage — Miri approves.
fn safe_raw_pointer() -> i32 {
    let mut x = 42;
    let ptr = &mut x as *mut i32;
    unsafe {
        *ptr = 100;
        *ptr
    }
}

/// Safe manual Vec-like allocation.
fn safe_manual_alloc() -> Vec<i32> {
    use std::alloc::{alloc, dealloc, Layout};

    let layout = Layout::array::<i32>(4).unwrap();
    let ptr = unsafe { alloc(layout) as *mut i32 };

    unsafe {
        for i in 0..4 {
            ptr.add(i).write(i as i32 * 10);
        }

        let result: Vec<i32> = (0..4).map(|i| ptr.add(i).read()).collect();

        dealloc(ptr as *mut u8, layout);
        result
    }
}

// ===== UB EXAMPLES: Miri catches these =====

// Example 1: Use after free
// fn use_after_free() -> i32 {
//     let ptr: *const i32;
//     {
//         let x = Box::new(42);
//         ptr = &*x as *const i32;
//     } // x dropped here
//     unsafe { *ptr } // UB: dangling pointer
//     // Miri error: dereferencing pointer to deallocated memory
// }

// Example 2: Out of bounds access
// fn out_of_bounds() -> i32 {
//     let arr = [1, 2, 3];
//     let ptr = arr.as_ptr();
//     unsafe { *ptr.add(5) } // UB: out of bounds
//     // Miri error: out-of-bounds pointer arithmetic
// }

// Example 3: Stacked Borrows violation
// fn stacked_borrows_violation() -> i32 {
//     let mut x = 42;
//     let r = &mut x;
//     let ptr = r as *mut i32;
//     let _val = *r;        // reborrow r, invalidates ptr under SB
//     unsafe { *ptr = 100 } // UB: ptr was invalidated
//     // Miri error: attempting to use invalidated tag
//     x
// }

// Example 4: Creating invalid reference
// fn misaligned_reference() {
//     let data = [0u8; 8];
//     let ptr = data.as_ptr().wrapping_add(1);
//     let _ref: &u32 = unsafe { &*(ptr as *const u32) }; // UB: misaligned
//     // Miri error: accessing memory with alignment 1, but u32 requires 4
// }

// ===== CORRECT alternatives =====

/// Correct version of stacked borrows pattern.
fn correct_raw_pointer_usage() -> i32 {
    let mut x = 42;
    let ptr = &mut x as *mut i32;
    // Don't use &mut x after creating ptr — use ptr exclusively
    unsafe {
        *ptr = 100;
        *ptr  // read through same pointer
    }
}

/// Safe transmute pattern for aligned data.
fn safe_aligned_cast() -> u32 {
    #[repr(C, align(4))]
    struct Aligned {
        data: [u8; 4],
    }

    let aligned = Aligned {
        data: [0x42, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00],
    };
    // Safe: properly aligned
    let value: u32 = unsafe { *(aligned.data.as_ptr() as *const u32) };
    value
}

/// Correct manual linked list node management.
struct Node {
    value: i32,
    next: Option<Box<Node>>,
}

fn safe_linked_list() -> Vec<i32> {
    let mut head = Some(Box::new(Node {
        value: 1,
        next: Some(Box::new(Node {
            value: 2,
            next: Some(Box::new(Node {
                value: 3,
                next: None,
            })),
        })),
    }));

    let mut result = Vec::new();
    let mut current = &head;
    while let Some(node) = current {
        result.push(node.value);
        current = &node.next;
    }
    drop(head);
    result
}

// ===== Tests structured for Miri =====

#[cfg(test)]
mod tests {
    use super::*;

    #[test]
    fn test_safe_raw_pointer() {
        assert_eq!(safe_raw_pointer(), 100);
    }

    #[test]
    fn test_safe_manual_alloc() {
        let v = safe_manual_alloc();
        assert_eq!(v, vec![0, 10, 20, 30]);
    }

    #[test]
    fn test_correct_raw_pointer() {
        assert_eq!(correct_raw_pointer_usage(), 100);
    }

    #[test]
    fn test_aligned_cast() {
        let val = safe_aligned_cast();
        assert_eq!(val, 0x42); // little-endian
    }

    #[test]
    fn test_linked_list() {
        assert_eq!(safe_linked_list(), vec![1, 2, 3]);
    }

    // Test that exercises interior mutability (Miri checks UnsafeCell)
    #[test]
    fn test_cell_safety() {
        use std::cell::UnsafeCell;

        let cell = UnsafeCell::new(42);
        unsafe {
            *cell.get() = 100;
            assert_eq!(*cell.get(), 100);
        }
    }

    // Miri-specific test configuration
    #[test]
    #[cfg_attr(miri, ignore)]  // skip under Miri if too slow
    fn test_large_allocation() {
        let v: Vec<u8> = vec![0; 10_000_000];
        assert_eq!(v.len(), 10_000_000);
    }
}

fn main() {
    println!("=== Miri Debugging Patterns ===");
    println!("Safe raw pointer: {}", safe_raw_pointer());
    println!("Safe alloc: {:?}", safe_manual_alloc());
    println!("Correct raw pointer: {}", correct_raw_pointer_usage());
    println!("Aligned cast: 0x{:X}", safe_aligned_cast());
    println!("Linked list: {:?}", safe_linked_list());

    println!("\n=== How to run with Miri ===");
    println!("  cargo +nightly miri test");
    println!("  MIRIFLAGS='-Zmiri-backtrace=full' cargo +nightly miri test");
    println!("  MIRIFLAGS='-Zmiri-leak-check' cargo +nightly miri run");
}
```

instruction: Implement advanced Rust debugging techniques for lifetime issues: using cargo-expand to understand lifetime elision, NLL boundary analysis, and building helper types that make lifetime errors more readable.
output:
<think>
Lifetime debugging in Rust is challenging because the errors can be cryptic. Let me demonstrate practical techniques.

Key approaches:
1. cargo-expand: see what the compiler actually generates after lifetime elision
2. Named lifetimes to understand where constraints come from
3. Helper types that make lifetime relationships explicit
4. NLL (Non-Lexical Lifetimes) boundary analysis
5. The `'static` vs borrowed lifetime distinction
6. Variance and its impact on lifetimes

Common lifetime errors and their solutions:
- "borrowed value does not live long enough" — return value outlives the borrow
- "cannot return reference to local variable" — need to return owned data
- "lifetime mismatch" in trait implementations — trait signature constrains lifetimes
- "cannot infer appropriate lifetime" — need explicit annotations

For cargo-expand: it shows how fn f(x: &str) -> &str becomes fn f<'a>(x: &'a str) -> &'a str after elision. This helps understand what the compiler infers.

I'll also show:
- A BorrowTracker type that helps visualize borrow scopes
- Techniques for making trait objects work with lifetimes
- The variance rules and how they affect containers
</think>

```rust
#![allow(dead_code)]

// ===== Understanding lifetime elision with explicit annotations =====

// What the programmer writes:
fn first_word(s: &str) -> &str {
    s.split_whitespace().next().unwrap_or("")
}

// What cargo-expand reveals (after elision):
// fn first_word<'a>(s: &'a str) -> &'a str
// The return value is tied to the input's lifetime.

// Multiple references — elision doesn't apply, must be explicit:
fn longest<'a>(x: &'a str, y: &'a str) -> &'a str {
    if x.len() > y.len() { x } else { y }
}

// Common error: trying to return a reference to different lifetimes
// fn bad_longest<'a, 'b>(x: &'a str, y: &'b str) -> &'a str {
//     if x.len() > y.len() { x } else { y } // ERROR: y has lifetime 'b, not 'a
// }

// ===== Helper types for explicit lifetime relationships =====

/// Wrapper that documents the lifetime relationship.
/// "This value borrows from the source with lifetime 'src"
struct Borrowed<'src, T> {
    value: T,
    _source: std::marker::PhantomData<&'src ()>,
}

impl<'src, T> Borrowed<'src, T> {
    fn new(value: T) -> Self {
        Borrowed {
            value,
            _source: std::marker::PhantomData,
        }
    }

    fn get(&self) -> &T {
        &self.value
    }
}

/// Demonstrates the pattern: parse returns data borrowing from input
fn parse_header<'input>(data: &'input str) -> Borrowed<'input, (&'input str, &'input str)> {
    let mut parts = data.splitn(2, ':')
        .map(str::trim);
    let key = parts.next().unwrap_or("");
    let value = parts.next().unwrap_or("");
    Borrowed::new((key, value))
}

// ===== NLL boundary analysis =====

fn nll_demo() {
    let mut data = vec![1, 2, 3, 4, 5];

    // Pre-NLL, this wouldn't compile because `first` borrows `data`
    // for the entire scope. With NLL, the borrow ends at last use.
    let first = &data[0];
    println!("First: {}", first);
    // NLL: borrow of `data` ends here (last use of `first`)

    // Now we can mutate:
    data.push(6);
    println!("After push: {:?}", data);

    // NLL boundary example: the borrow ends at the last USE, not the scope
    let second = &data[1]; // immutable borrow starts
    let third = &data[2];  // another immutable borrow (compatible)
    println!("{} {}", second, third); // last use of both borrows
    // Both borrows end here (NLL)
    data.push(7); // OK — no outstanding borrows
}

// ===== Lifetime in structs — making it clear =====

/// Without documentation, it's unclear what this borrows:
struct ConfigBad<'a> {
    host: &'a str,
    port: u16,
}

/// Better: self-documenting with descriptive lifetime names
struct Config<'cfg_source> {
    host: &'cfg_source str,  // borrows from the config file content
    port: u16,               // owned, no lifetime needed
}

impl<'cfg_source> Config<'cfg_source> {
    fn from_str(source: &'cfg_source str) -> Self {
        // Parse from the source string — return borrows from source
        Config {
            host: source.split('=').nth(1).unwrap_or("localhost").trim(),
            port: 8080,
        }
    }
}

// ===== Trait objects with lifetimes =====

trait Processor {
    fn process(&self, data: &str) -> String;
}

struct Upper;
impl Processor for Upper {
    fn process(&self, data: &str) -> String {
        data.to_uppercase()
    }
}

// Common error: dyn Trait without lifetime bound
// fn bad_make_processor() -> Box<dyn Processor> { ... }
// Actually fine because Box<dyn Trait> implicitly has Box<dyn Trait + 'static>

// When the trait object borrows from something:
struct BorrowingProcessor<'a> {
    prefix: &'a str,
}

impl<'a> Processor for BorrowingProcessor<'a> {
    fn process(&self, data: &str) -> String {
        format!("{}: {}", self.prefix, data)
    }
}

// Must explicitly annotate the lifetime on the trait object:
fn make_borrowing_processor<'a>(prefix: &'a str) -> Box<dyn Processor + 'a> {
    Box::new(BorrowingProcessor { prefix })
}

// ===== Variance demonstration =====

// &'a T is covariant in 'a: can use a longer lifetime where shorter expected
// &'a mut T is invariant in T: cannot substitute different lifetimes

fn covariance_demo<'long>(long_ref: &'long str) {
    // Can pass &'long where &'short is expected (covariance)
    fn takes_short<'short>(s: &'short str) -> usize {
        s.len()
    }
    let _ = takes_short(long_ref); // 'long: 'short => OK
}

// ===== Debug helper: show borrow scopes =====

struct BorrowGuard<'a, T: std::fmt::Debug> {
    name: &'static str,
    _phantom: std::marker::PhantomData<&'a T>,
}

impl<'a, T: std::fmt::Debug> BorrowGuard<'a, T> {
    fn new(name: &'static str, _ref: &'a T) -> Self {
        println!("[BORROW START] {} at {:p}", name, _ref as *const T);
        BorrowGuard { name, _phantom: std::marker::PhantomData }
    }
}

impl<'a, T: std::fmt::Debug> Drop for BorrowGuard<'a, T> {
    fn drop(&mut self) {
        println!("[BORROW END]   {}", self.name);
    }
}

fn main() {
    println!("=== Lifetime Debugging Patterns ===\n");

    // NLL boundaries
    nll_demo();

    // Parse with explicit borrowing
    let raw = "Content-Type: application/json";
    let header = parse_header(raw);
    println!("\nParsed: {:?}", header.get());

    // Config borrowing from source
    let config_source = String::from("host=example.com");
    let config = Config::from_str(&config_source);
    println!("Host: {}", config.host);

    // Borrow visualization
    println!("\n=== Borrow Scopes ===");
    let data = vec![1, 2, 3];
    {
        let _guard = BorrowGuard::new("data_ref", &data);
        println!("Using data: {:?}", data);
    } // guard drops, showing borrow end

    // Trait objects
    let prefix = String::from("LOG");
    let processor = make_borrowing_processor(&prefix);
    println!("\n{}", processor.process("test message"));

    println!("\n=== cargo-expand usage ===");
    println!("cargo expand --lib           # see all expanded macros + lifetimes");
    println!("cargo expand fn_name         # expand specific item");
}
```

instruction: Demonstrate the tracing crate ecosystem in Rust for production observability: setting up subscribers, creating spans with structured fields, async-aware instrumentation, and integrating with OpenTelemetry.
output:
<think>
The tracing ecosystem is Rust's standard approach to production observability. It's far more than logging — it provides structured, contextual diagnostics.

Core concepts:
1. Spans: represent a unit of work (function call, request handling). They have enter/exit.
2. Events: discrete occurrences within a span (like log messages)
3. Subscribers: process and output spans/events
4. Layers: composable subscribers (stdout + file + OpenTelemetry)
5. Fields: structured key-value data on spans and events

Key crates:
- tracing: core instrumentation API
- tracing-subscriber: subscriber implementations
- tracing-appender: non-blocking file output
- tracing-opentelemetry: OpenTelemetry integration
- tracing-futures: async instrument support

I'll show:
1. Full subscriber setup with layers
2. #[instrument] for automatic span creation
3. Structured fields that propagate through the call stack
4. Async-aware spans that correctly handle .await points
5. Custom span events for business logic
6. Error handling integration

Since this is training data, I'll include the actual tracing crate usage (not simulated), but structure it so it compiles standalone with the right dependencies.
</think>

```rust
// Cargo.toml:
// [dependencies]
// tracing = "0.1"
// tracing-subscriber = { version = "0.3", features = ["env-filter", "json"] }
// tokio = { version = "1", features = ["full"] }
// uuid = { version = "1", features = ["v4"] }

use std::time::Duration;

// Using tracing macros
// In real code: use tracing::{info, warn, error, debug, trace, instrument, span, Level};

// ===== Subscriber setup =====

fn init_subscriber() {
    // In production:
    /*
    use tracing_subscriber::{fmt, EnvFilter, layer::SubscriberExt, util::SubscriberInitExt};

    // Console layer with colors and timing
    let console_layer = fmt::layer()
        .with_target(true)
        .with_thread_ids(true)
        .with_file(true)
        .with_line_number(true)
        .with_span_events(fmt::format::FmtSpan::CLOSE); // log span duration

    // JSON layer for machine parsing (e.g., log aggregation)
    let json_layer = fmt::layer()
        .json()
        .with_span_list(true);

    // Environment-based filtering: RUST_LOG=info,my_app=debug
    let filter = EnvFilter::try_from_default_env()
        .unwrap_or_else(|_| EnvFilter::new("info,my_app=debug"));

    // File appender (non-blocking)
    let file_appender = tracing_appender::rolling::daily("/var/log/myapp", "app.log");
    let (non_blocking, _guard) = tracing_appender::non_blocking(file_appender);
    let file_layer = fmt::layer()
        .with_writer(non_blocking)
        .json();

    tracing_subscriber::registry()
        .with(filter)
        .with(console_layer)
        // .with(file_layer)
        .init();
    */

    println!("[Subscriber initialized]");
}

// ===== Instrumented service =====

// In real code, these would use #[tracing::instrument]
// #[instrument(skip(db), fields(request_id = %request_id))]
fn handle_request(request_id: &str, path: &str) -> Result<String, AppError> {
    println!("[SPAN] handle_request request_id={} path={}", request_id, path);

    // tracing::info!(path, "Processing request");
    println!("  [INFO] Processing request path={}", path);

    let user = authenticate(request_id)?;
    println!("  [DEBUG] Authenticated user={}", user);

    let data = fetch_data(path)?;
    println!("  [INFO] Fetched data size={}", data.len());

    Ok(data)
}

// #[instrument(level = "debug")]
fn authenticate(request_id: &str) -> Result<String, AppError> {
    println!("  [SPAN] authenticate");
    if request_id.starts_with("bad") {
        // tracing::warn!(request_id, "Authentication failed");
        println!("  [WARN] Authentication failed request_id={}", request_id);
        return Err(AppError::Unauthorized);
    }
    Ok("user-42".to_string())
}

// #[instrument(fields(bytes_read))]
fn fetch_data(path: &str) -> Result<String, AppError> {
    println!("  [SPAN] fetch_data path={}", path);

    if path.contains("error") {
        // tracing::error!(path, "Resource not found");
        println!("  [ERROR] Resource not found path={}", path);
        return Err(AppError::NotFound(path.to_string()));
    }

    let data = format!("response data for {}", path);
    // Span::current().record("bytes_read", data.len());
    println!("  [SPAN] fetch_data bytes_read={}", data.len());
    Ok(data)
}

// ===== Async instrumentation =====

// In real async code:
// #[instrument(skip(self), fields(task_id = %self.id))]
async fn process_batch(items: Vec<String>) -> Vec<Result<String, AppError>> {
    println!("[SPAN] process_batch items={}", items.len());

    let mut results = Vec::new();
    for (i, item) in items.iter().enumerate() {
        // tracing::info!(item_index = i, "Processing item");
        println!("  [INFO] Processing item item_index={}", i);

        // .instrument(tracing::info_span!("process_item", index = i))
        let result = process_single_item(item).await;
        results.push(result);
    }

    // tracing::info!(success_count = results.iter().filter(|r| r.is_ok()).count(),
    //                "Batch complete");
    let success = results.iter().filter(|r| r.is_ok()).count();
    println!("  [INFO] Batch complete success_count={}", success);

    results
}

async fn process_single_item(item: &str) -> Result<String, AppError> {
    // Simulate async I/O
    tokio::time::sleep(Duration::from_millis(10)).await;
    Ok(format!("processed: {}", item))
}

// ===== Error integration =====

#[derive(Debug)]
enum AppError {
    NotFound(String),
    Unauthorized,
    Internal(String),
}

impl std::fmt::Display for AppError {
    fn fmt(&self, f: &mut std::fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> std::fmt::Result {
        match self {
            AppError::NotFound(r) => write!(f, "not found: {}", r),
            AppError::Unauthorized => write!(f, "unauthorized"),
            AppError::Internal(msg) => write!(f, "internal: {}", msg),
        }
    }
}

impl std::error::Error for AppError {}

// ===== OpenTelemetry integration pattern =====

/*
// Real OpenTelemetry setup:
fn init_otel_tracing() {
    use opentelemetry::sdk::trace::TracerProvider;
    use tracing_opentelemetry::OpenTelemetryLayer;

    let tracer = opentelemetry_otlp::new_pipeline()
        .tracing()
        .with_exporter(opentelemetry_otlp::new_exporter().tonic())
        .install_batch(opentelemetry::runtime::Tokio)
        .expect("Failed to init OTel tracer");

    let otel_layer = OpenTelemetryLayer::new(tracer);

    tracing_subscriber::registry()
        .with(EnvFilter::new("info"))
        .with(fmt::layer())
        .with(otel_layer)  // traces go to OTel collector
        .init();
}
*/

#[tokio::main]
async fn main() {
    init_subscriber();

    println!("\n=== Sync request handling ===");
    match handle_request("req-001", "/api/users") {
        Ok(data) => println!("Success: {}\n", data),
        Err(e) => println!("Error: {}\n", e),
    }

    match handle_request("req-002", "/api/error/missing") {
        Ok(data) => println!("Success: {}\n", data),
        Err(e) => println!("Error: {}\n", e),
    }

    println!("=== Async batch processing ===");
    let items = vec!["item-a".into(), "item-b".into(), "item-c".into()];
    let results = process_batch(items).await;
    println!("Results: {} items processed\n", results.len());

    println!("=== Key tracing patterns ===");
    println!("1. #[instrument] on every public function");
    println!("2. Structured fields: info!(user_id = %id, \"message\")");
    println!("3. .instrument(span) for async tasks");
    println!("4. Span::current().record() for deferred fields");
    println!("5. EnvFilter for runtime log level control");
}
```
